coredata.dc:title: Quasi-molecular hydrogen storage capacity of graphene quantum dots: A dispersion corrected DFT study
coredata.dc:creator: Khadilkar, Pranav
Samudre, Nikhil S.
Krishnamurty, Sailaja
coredata.dc:description: Efficient storage of hydrogen, green fuel with the highest energy density, remains a pressing challenge. Among the several materials investigated for their potential hydrogen storage, 2D materials, like graphene, have advantages such as mechanical strength and large surface area but fail to store hydrogen reversibly. In this context, the present computational experiment demonstrates the potential of Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) with 24, 40 and 42 carbon atoms for their hydrogen storage capacity in quasi-molecular mode. Factors such as edge terminations, heteroatom doping, and anchoring of metal atoms are evaluated as a function of their storage capacity. The study clearly demonstrates an enhanced storage capacity of quantum dots, particularly, when a single Ti adatom is anchored on a 24 carbon atom GQD with a storage weight % of 2.24 % w/w. The storage weight % is further noted to increase as a function of the number of Ti atoms anchored on the GQD with the highest hydrogen storage weight % of 6.1 % w/w. Importantly, the adsorption of hydrogen molecule on the Ti atom is through a quasi-molecular mode and is driven by Kubas interaction. This type of interactions makes GQDs as viable storage materials at room temperature. Secondly, the work demonstrates that GQDs offer higher storage capacities of hydrogen molecules as compared to their 2D counterparts viz., graphene sheets, making them attractive candidates to be explored experimentally.

head.ce:title: Quasi-molecular hydrogen storage capacity of graphene quantum dots: A dispersion corrected DFT study

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Efficient storage of hydrogen, green fuel with the highest energy density, remains a pressing challenge. Among the several materials investigated for their potential hydrogen storage, 2D materials, like graphene, have advantages such as mechanical strength and large surface area but fail to store hydrogen reversibly. In this context, the present computational experiment demonstrates the potential of Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) with 24, 40 and 42 carbon atoms for their hydrogen storage capacity in quasi-molecular mode. Factors such as edge terminations, heteroatom doping, and anchoring of metal atoms are evaluated as a function of their storage capacity. The study clearly demonstrates an enhanced storage capacity of quantum dots, particularly, when a single Ti adatom is anchored on a 24 carbon atom GQD with a storage weight % of 2.24 % w/w. The storage weight % is further noted to increase as a function of the number of Ti atoms anchored on the GQD with the highest hydrogen storage weight % of 6.1 % w/w. Importantly, the adsorption of hydrogen molecule on the Ti atom is through a quasi-molecular mode and is driven by Kubas interaction. This type of interactions makes GQDs as viable storage materials at room temperature. Secondly, the work demonstrates that GQDs offer higher storage capacities of hydrogen molecules as compared to their 2D counterparts viz., graphene sheets, making them attractive candidates to be explored experimentally.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Perils in the usage of fossil fuels as a source of energy are now well established [1] and green hydrogen has been accepted as the most promising alternative to fossil fuels [2–7]. However, practical implementation of green hydrogen as a fuel requires a sustainable and economical production of hydrogen [8–12] and it's safe storage [13–19]. While, creditable, advancements are being made towards economical production of hydrogen through water electrolysis, its far reaching usage as fuel will eventually depend on its portability through hydrogen storage materials. In that context, research works have come out with wide range of materials as promising hydrogen storage materials, Liquid Organic Hydro-Carbons, high entropy alloys, metal hydrides, porous materials and carbon based materials to name a few [20–30].

Hydrogen is stored in all of the above materials either through physical adsorption (physisorption, where hydrogen is in the gas form) or chemical adsorption (chemisorption) [31,32]. It has been identified that the physisorption energy for reversible storage of hydrogen is in meV range wherein hydrogen molecule is adsorbed through van-der Waals interactions [33]. On the other hand, adsorption energies between −2.0 eV to −4.0 eV account for chemisorption of H2 molecule which dissociates into individual atoms [33]. Physisorption is generally preferred than chemisorption as it is easy to recover the physisorbed hydrogen but mandates very high operating pressures, thereby nulling the advantage. Conversely, storage in chemisorption mode mandates very high energy requirements for dehydrogenation. Of late, a third mode of hydrogen adsorption is identified wherein the bond between H atoms in an H2 molecule is only weakened without dissociating the molecule. The binding strength in these cases is intermediate between physisorption and chemisorption and lies in the range of −0.2 eV to −0.7 eV. This quasi-molecular bonding is accompanied by a charge transfer between the hydrogen molecules and the storage materials and is reportedly ideal for hydrogen storage under ambient pressures and temperatures [33].

However, storage of hydrogen in a quasi-molecular bonding state, mandates large volume based materials that are robust and thermodynamically stable materials. In the context of above two points, carbon based materials (such as graphene, carbon nanotubes and fullerenes) stand an excellent chance on account of their high thermodynamic/mechanical stability followed by their light weight and have been widely explored by various experimental and theoretical groups [25–42]. Among the three types of carbon based architectures, graphene has the highest surface area with a remarkable tensile and mechanical strength, good electrical conductivity and transparency. Most significantly it is an environment friendly, lightweight material and hence transportable over long distances. However, theoretical investigations have shown that hydrogen molecules adsorb on pristine graphene with an adsorption energy of −0.01 to −0.09 eV, (essentially are physisorbed on graphene) making it unsuitable as a reversible storage material for hydrogen [36]. However, on surface modifications such as heteroatom doping, adatom anchoring, functionalization and defect induction within the surface, graphene sheets have shown promise with improvement in their hydrogen storage ability. The band gap in graphene is initially modulated by dopants due to their impact on the Fermi level behaviour. In the case of boron doping, boron is electron-deficient compared to carbon. This electron deficiency leads to the creation of holes in the graphene structure, resulting in a breaking of symmetry. Consequently, graphene is transformed into a p-type semiconductor. This shift in the Fermi level, accompanied by increase in the band gap. In the context of nitrogen doping, nitrogen possesses a lone pair of electrons. This lone pair makes graphene electron-rich, Transforming it into an n-type semiconductor results in a reduction of the band gap in graphene [43–49].

In this context, another modification of graphene, viz., its quantum confinement has demonstrated excellent potential for adsorption/sensing of molecules and gases experimentally [50–54]. These quantumly confined graphene based materials called as Poly Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAHs) or Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) demonstrate enhanced catalytic activity, electronic and magnetic properties as demonstrated by experimental and theoretical studies [55–59]. Graphene quantum dots being extensively π-conjugated systems have far reaching changes on doping or adding ad atom by changing their hybridisation properties. Several recent synthesis accounts of graphene quantum dots also make them as practically viable with an excellent potential for various applications [60–67].

Motivated by these facts and that there are only a couple of research works that report the potential of GQDs as hydrogen storage materials [68,69], the present work explores the potential of GQDs of finite sizes for storage of hydrogen molecules in quasi-molecular state. The interactions of GQDs with hydrogen molecules (H2) are studied using the first principles density functional theory with van der Waals correction for dispersion energy (DFT + vdW). The GQDs with 24, 40 and 42 atoms are considered for the study. Further, the effect of tweaking the chemical composition of a GQDs by doping or adding an ad-atom towards the adsorption of molecular hydrogen is also evaluated and a favourable system for hydrogen storage is suggested at the end of the study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Models considered for their hydrogen storage capacity

ce:section.ce:para: GQDs of three different sizes, GQDm (where m = 24, 40 and 42 carbon atoms (as shown in Fig. 1(a)) are considered for the study. These finite sized systems are well known to have distinct edge terminations referred to as zig-zag edge termination and armchair edge termination [69–72]. In the current work, we have evaluated the potential of molecules with zig-zag edges for their hydrogen storage capacity on account of their higher thermal (and thereby mechanical) stability as compared to the armchair conformations [71–73]. The unsaturated edges of GQDs are saturated by H and OH functional groups (hereafter referred to as f-GQDm, where f = H and OH). OH functionalized GQDs are considered to check the effect of an electronegative function group on adsorption of hydrogen molecules. The choice functionalization is done based on previously reported literature, OH is selected since it is most widely reported experimentally [73].

GQDs are doped with two different heteroatoms viz., boron and nitrogen for both H and OH terminations. Doping of graphene based surfaces with these two elements is well known for modifying adsorption properties of the graphene sheets [74]. The doping is increased sequentially till the maximum doping for which the graphene sheet is found to remain planar is identified. In other words, a single dopant atom of N/B is introduced and the number of dopant atoms increased till the GQDs is noted to remain stable i.e. retain its nearly flat orientation without formation of defect sites and dangling bonds. This is because, when graphene based systems change from planar non defective conformation to non-planar orientations with defects the carbon atoms form sp3 hybridised orbitals with the neighbouring atoms making them more chemically reactive and is likely to form chemical bonds with hydrogen (amounting to chemisorption) [75]. The GQDs are seen to not to form curved structures with defects for upto 9 % nitrogen doping and 25 % boron doping. Hence, further hydrogen adsorption studies are limited to exploring the GQDs with maximum dopant concentration (i.e., 9 % for nitrogen doping and 25 % for boron doping) for their hydrogen storage capacity.

In addition to the doping of the nanoflakes with boron and nitrogen, doping of the nanoflakes with metal atoms such as Al, Ti and Zn is also considered. Such embedding/doping is reported to be feasible within 2D graphene sheets and GQDs using experimental methods such as electron beam manipulation [76]. Of the three metal atoms doping considered in the study, only Al and Ti atom dope favourably without formation of further defects within the carbon rings.

GQDs are chemically modified by anchoring adatoms viz., Al, Ti and Zn on them. The choice of the adatom is based on the literature reports that these elements demonstrate high storage capacities for hydrogen [77–80]. It is noted that introduction of adatom on the dopant induced GQDs is not thermodynamically stable with the structure getting disoriented or adatom getting desorbed from the structure. However, adatoms remained stably anchored on pristine GQDs. Out of the adatoms considered in the study only Al and Ti atoms adsorb on the GQDs and Zn atoms desorb from all the nanoflakes considered in the study. Thus, the obtained studies are then compared with the earlier reported Mg adatom decorated graphene nanoflake study from the literature [68].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrogen adsorption on all the above models of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Pristine, doped with B, N, Ti, and Al and Ti/Al anchored GQDs with H and OH terminations are further explored for their hydrogen storage capacity.

Hydrogen molecule is adsorbed in three different orientations on the surface atoms of the GQD: (a) Parallel to GQD over the hollow region (referred to as centre (C) orientation), (b) Over carbon‑carbon edge and parallel to GQD (referred to as edge (E) orientation) (c) Over hollow region and perpendicular to GQD (referred to as perpendicular (P) orientation). The three orientations are shown in Fig. 1(b). The possibility of H2 molecule adsorption along the terminal atoms of the carbon along these three orientations was also explored and it was noted that for all the models studies, the H2 molecule moves over to the surface of the GQD surface which shows that the favourable position for H2 adsorption is over the GQD surface.

Following this, the number of hydrogen molecules adsorbed on the GQD surface in all the three orientations is increased from 1 to the saturation limit (the limit where the interaction of the additional hydrogen molecule does not fall below −0.2 eV which is the minimum limit of the quasimolecular interaction) [33,45]. The maximum hydrogen storage limit for each structure is identified and the storage capacities are quantified using various electronic parameters.
The nomenclature for all the computational models considered in the study are summarised in Table 1 .

All the simulations are done using Density Functional Theory (DFT) [81] as implemented in the deMon2k package for finite sized systems [82]. To expand the electronic wavefunction the double-zeta valence polarised (DZVP) basis set is used for all the elements [83]. The accuracy of the DZVP basis set in accurately determining the structure-property relationship of these systems was validated in one of our earlier research works [69]. The exchange-correlation effects of the many body system are considered using generalised gradient approximation as implemented within the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) functional [84,85]. The choice of the functional is once again based on the fact that this has been earlier reported to have predicted accurate physico-chemical properties of graphene based quantum dots [69,86]. The dispersion correction to the DFT method [87] is used as implemented in the deMon2k code [88] to take into account the London dispersion forces [89]. The alignment of geometries depends on gradient and displacement criteria, utilizing a threshold of 10−5 au for geometry optimization and 10−8 au for achieving convergence of SCF cycles. For all systems, the lowest spin states are considered for the study. For GQDs, singlet spin states the Restricted Kohn Sham (RKS) formalism is applied, while the Restricted Open Kohn Sham (ROKS) formalism is employed for doublet spin states. In case of the Ti doped GQDs electronic structure analysis is done to understand the nature of interaction between Ti atom and H2 molecules. Charge transfer between the Ti atom and the H2 molecule is evaluated using Mulliken population analysis [90] as implemented in the deMon2k software package.

In the case of metal atom-decorated GQDs, the adsorption energy(Eads) of metal atoms on the GQD surface is calculated using Eq. (1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: H2 adsorption on a pristine and doped f-GQD24
ce:section.ce:para: 24 atom GQD also known as superbenzene and cyclobenzene (shown in Fig. 1(a)) is the first system we evaluate for its potential hydrogen storage property. This molecule, referred to as coronene, is also used as a precursor for the synthesis of graphene, metal organic frameworks and has been further explored by theoretical researchers for its hydrogen storage capacity [93,94]. 24 atom GQD (shown in Fig. 1(a1)) is doped with B, N, Al, and Ti (where one of the carbon atom is replaced in the inside ring with the dopant element) as mentioned in the Section 2.1.2. As a first step, we identify the stability of thus doped GQDs by calculating the formation energy (evaluated through Eq. 6 in Section 2.2). In Table 2 , we give the formation energies of the doped systems. The values in the Table 2 clearly indicated that all the formation energies are negative which is indicative of the fact that they are thermodynamically stable.

Having identified their stability/feasibility of their formation through exothermic formation energies, a single hydrogen molecule is adsorbed on pristine and doped 24 atom GQDs. In case the adsorption of the first H2 molecule is favourable, second H2 molecule is further adsorbed. The number of H2 molecules are then sequentially increased till a saturation limit of quasi-molecular adsorption is identified for each system. Orientations of pristine f-GQDs24 before and after hydrogen molecule adsorption are given in Supplementary Fig. S.F.1. The corresponding adsorption energies are compared in Fig. 2 and absolute values for the same are given in Supplementary Table S.T.1. It is noted from Fig. 2, that the adsorption of H2 molecule on pristine f-GQD is characterised by energy values less than 0.05 eV, clearly falling in the physisorption range (and not in the quasi-molecular adsorption range). This is true for all orientations of H2 molecule adsorption viz., centre (C) orientation, edge (E) orientation and perpendicular (P) on H-GQDs and OH-GQDs. This suggests that the pristine GQD is not suitable for reversible H2 storage at ambient pressures and temperatures.

Doped 24 atom GQDs are next evaluated for their hydrogen storage. The adsorption energies of H2 on boron doped f-GQDs (see Supplementary Fig. S.F.2 for optimised conformations) are in the range ≈ −0.05 eV. Nitrogen doped GQDs24 (see Supplementary Fig. S.F.3) demonstrates adsorption energies of ≈ −0.06 eV for H-GQD24. Nitrogen doped OH-GQDs24 demonstrate a slightly better adsorption energies of around −0.09 eV. In case of Al doping, on account of larger atomic radius of Al than carbon, Al atom is pushed slightly out of plane causing a marginal curvature in f-GQD24 (see Supplementary Fig. S.F.4). The corresponding adsorption energies of molecular hydrogen on Al doped f-GQDs24 are also shown in Fig. 2. The Al doped H-GQDs24 have improved adsorption energies towards molecular hydrogen, reaching to nearly −0.15 eV, just short of quasi-molecular hydrogen adsorption values. On the other hand, Ti doped f-GQDs24 demonstrate more favourable adsorption energies towards the H2 molecule with adsorption energies ∼ −0.2 eV. As mentioned earlier, this is in the required range for a quasi-molecular adsorption which is −0.2 to −0.7 eV as highlighted using a green line in Fig. 2. The Ti doped OH-GQD24 demonstrates higher adsorption energies for molecular hydrogen (around −0.24 eV) as compared to its H terminated counterpart (which is around −0.2 eV). The most favourable optimised conformations of Ti doped f-GQDs24 are shown in Supplementary Fig. S.F.5.

The quasi molecular adsorption on Ti doped GQDs is accompanied by an elongation in the HH bond from 0.75 Å to 0.77 Å. The total charge on the hydrogen molecule after adsorption is 0.08e for H-GQD24 and 0.09e for OH-GQD24 (See Supplementary Fig. S.F.6 for noting the marginal charge transfer in case of Ti doped GQDs as compared to the other doped GQDs). The IR stretching of the adsorbed H2 molecule on Ti doped H-GQD24 is 4059.4 cm−1 and that for OH-GQD24 is 3895.3 cm−1. This slight decrease in the stretching frequency as compared to its value in free standing H2 molecule (4400 cm−1) is concurrent with the small increase of the HH bond length and indicative of an activated HH bond and thereby quasi-molecular hydrogen adsorption. However, the second hydrogen molecule adsorbs onto the Ti doped H-GQD24 and OH-GQD24 with energies −0.17 eV and − 0.12 eV, respectively with no change on the H2 bond length (the bond length remains around 0.75 Å). Thus, the adsorption energy values, corroborated with the change in the hydrogen bond and charge transfer to it all establish that Ti doped f-GQD24 can reversibly store H2 molecule in the quasi-molecular mode, albeit restricted to only one molecule.

ce:section.ce:section-title: H2 adsorption on pristine and doped f-GQD40
ce:section.ce:para: In one of their systematic studies on graphene quantum dots, Peeters and co-workers have demonstrated a 40 atom GQD to have the largest dipole moment [95] and it is interesting to note if this property results in a more favourable H2 adsorption. Hence, pristine and doped H-GQD40 and OH-GQD40 are evaluated for their H2 adsorption potential. While the pristine GQDs are well known, doped GQDs are more sparsely reported and hence it is pertinent to evaluate their formation energies. The formation energies of doping a f-GQD40 with nitrogen atoms, boron atoms, single Al/Ti atoms are calculated and reported in Table 3 . All the formation energies are negative suggesting that the doped systems are energetically stable. The conformations of pristine f-GQDs40 before and after the adsorption of H2 molecules are shown in Supplementary Fig. S.F.7. The adsorption energies of H2 molecule on pristine H-GQD40 and OH-GQD40 are ≈ −0.06 eV and ≈ −0.043 eV, respectively. The corresponding adsorption energies are compared in Fig. 3 and absolute values for the same are given in Supplementary Table S.T.2. 25 % boron doped (i.e. when 10 carbon atoms are replaced with boron) conformations before and after the hydrogen molecule adsorption are shown in Supplementary Fig. S.F.8. The adsorption energies for these cases are in the range of ≈ −0.05 to −0.15 eV (see Fig. 3) for all the cases with the exception of perpendicular orientation of H2 molecule on 25 % boron doped OH-GQD40. 9 % nitrogen doped conformations (i.e. where 4 carbon atoms are replaced with nitrogen as shown in supplementary Fig. S.F.9) on the other hand show an improved molecular hydrogen adsorption energy (of about −0.1 eV) for H-GQDs40 through C and P orientations.

The Al atom doped GQDs (structural orientations elucidated in Supplementary Fig. S.F.10) demonstrate better adsorption energies for H-GQD40, which fall around −0.13 eV. As was noted for the cases of f-GQDs24. Al atom doping leads to a marginal loss of planarity on account of its larger atomic radius as compared to carbon (See supplementary Fig. S.F.10). The loss of planarity is quantified through evaluation of εpro, a shape parameter defined as follows.
(7)
ε pro
=
2
Q 1
Q 2
+
Q 3
where Q1 ≥ Q2 ≥ Q3 are the eigenvalues of the quadrupole tensor Qij = ΣRLiRLj with RLi being the ith coordinate of the ion I relative to the centre of mass of the GQD. Supplementary Fig. S.F.11 gives the εpro for N doped, B doped, Al doped and Ti doped system as compared to the pristine 40 atom GQD. The εpro values deviate the most for the Al doped H terminated and OH terminated GQDs. For the Ti doped GQD, the adsorption energies of H2 on H-GQD40 and OH-GQDs40 range between −0.18 and − 0.20 as seen from Fig. 3. The HH bond length in hydrogen molecule increases from 0.75 to 0.77 Å upon its adsorption on Ti doped f-GQDs40. Also there is a charge transfer of 0.078e to the H2 molecule for H-GQDs40 and 0.076e for OH-GQDs40. The IR stretching frequency of the HH bond length is 4015.4 cm−1 for Ti doped H-GQD40 and 3968.7 for OH-GQD40 cm−1, less than the frequency of free standing H2 molecule. This is once again suggestive of quasi-molecular adsorption. As in case of Al atom, Ti atom doping leads to a marginal structural deformation on account of its larger atomic radius shown in Supplementary Fig. S.F.12. Thus, the trend of Ti atom doping leading to favourable quasi molecular H2 adsorption on GQDs is consistently noted for also f-GQD40. Further, as in case of f-GQDs24, second hydrogen molecule adsorbs on the f-GQD40 with lower adsorption energies ranging between −0.10 eV to −0.12 eV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: H2 adsorption on pristine and doped f-GQD42
ce:section.ce:para: Next we have studied the hydrogen storage potential of f-GQD42.This molecule referred to as hexaperi-hexabenzocoronene is a molecule that has been synthesised experimentally and exploited for its optical properties and reactivity on account of its excellent electron transfer properties, a property that is relevant when we desire quasi molecular adsorption of gases [96–98]. Pristine and doped f-GQDs42 are evaluated for the H2 adsorption. Before that we screen the stability of the doped GQDs42 and Table 4 gives the formation energies of doped systems. From the table it can be seen that the doping is energetically stable as all the formation energies are negative. A relative comparison of doping N, B, Al and Ti in GQDs of various sizes reveals that N atom in H-GQD40 is the most stable (Supplementary Fig. S.F.13). The adsorption energies of molecular hydrogen on f-GQDs42 are given in Fig. 4 and absolute values for the same are given in Supplementary Table S.T.3. The corresponding geometries before and after hydrogen adsorption on f-GQDs42 (pristine, boron doped, nitrogen doped, Al doped and Ti doped) are given in Supplementary Figs. S.F.14, S.F.15, S.F.16, S.F.17 and S.F.18 respectively. Once again, the adsorption energies of H2 molecule fall in the quasi-molecular range in case of Ti doped f-GQDs42. Also, similar to previous two sizes evaluated, on adsorption, an increase in HH bond length to 0.77 Å accompanied by a charge transfer of ≈ 0.08e to the hydrogen molecule. The IR stretching frequency of the HH bond is 4000 cm−1 for Ti doped H-GQD42 and 3980 cm−1 for OH-GQD42. Similar to the previous two cases this frequency is less than that of free standing H2 molecules. Notably, nitrogen doped OH-GQDs42 show a remarkable improvement in their quasi- molecular hydrogen adsorption energies of nearly −0.18 eV. Al doped H-GQDs42 adsorb hydrogen molecule with values around −0.125 eV. Apart from this, rest of the systems demonstrate a physisorption towards molecular hydrogen with adsorption energies below −0.1 eV. Second hydrogen molecule adsorb on the Ti doped f-GQDs42 with adsorption energies between −0.12 to −0.13 eV there by validating the fact that irrespective of the size, the Ti doped f-GQDs can adsorb only one hydrogen molecule in quasi molecular mode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: H2 adsorption on a single Al and Ti adatom decorated f-GQDsm (m = 24,40,42)

ce:section.ce:para: Analysis of the adsorption energies in the previous three sections has demonstrated that doping of GQDs with Ti (irrespective of the edge termination groups) has improved the adsorption energies of molecular hydrogen from physisorption range values to the quasi-molecular adsorption range values (−0.2 eV to −0.7 eV) for any given GQD. These adsorption energies are accompanied by corresponding weakening of the hydrogen bond and a charge (−ve) transfer to it from the GQD compounded by a red shift in the IR stretching frequency of the H2 molecule. However, it is pertinent to note that as mentioned above that the adsorption of a second hydrogen molecule is not favourable in these cases limiting the storage weight percentage of these nanoflakes.

Reckoning on the fact that doping with an adatom increases the electron donation ability of the GQD, we have next attempted to anchor Al and Ti atoms as adatoms on GQDs. In all the cases, the adatom is placed at the centre of the carbon ring as demonstrated in Supplementary Fig. S.F.19 (a). Al adatom anchored on f-GQD24, f-GQD40 and f-GQD42 before hydrogen molecule adsorption are shown in Supplementary Figs. S.F.19 (a and b), S.F.20 (a and b), S.F.21 (a and b), respectively. In some cases, such as Supplementary Fig. S.F.19 (b), the Al adatom moves to the edge of the GQD on optimization. The adsorption energy of the Al atom on the f-GQDm is calculated using Eq. (1) and the adsorption energy values for the Al as adatom on GQDs of all studied sizes are mentioned in Table 5 . From the table it is seen that the Al atom adsorbs weakly on all GQD surfaces, the Al atom shows highest adsorption energy for H-GQD40 and the lowest for H-GQD24. H2 molecular adsorption is evaluated on all the optimised Al adatom-GQD complexes and the final H2 adsorbed orientations are shown in Supplementary Figs. S.F.19-S.F.21 (c) and (d). The values in Table 6 , clearly demonstrate that the hydrogen molecule is only physisorbed on the Al adatom based GQDs.

Since, the adsorption of the first H2 molecule resulted in a chemisorption, further hydrogen molecules were added one by one to the system. The final optimised conformations for these complexes are elucidated in Fig. 5 (a2, a3, a4) for H- terminated GQDs and Fig. 5 (b2, b3, b4) for OH-terminated GQDs. The adsorption energy values for these molecules are represented in Fig. 6(a). It is clearly noted from Fig. 6(a) that the second, third and fourth hydrogen molecules adsorb with energy values(
E ads
nH 2
) of −0.59 eV, −0.48 eV and − 0.38 eV, respectively. From the figure it is clear that the change in adsorption energy with the number of hydrogen molecules adsorbed is similar for H-GQD24 and OH-GQD24. In the process of adsorption for first incoming H2 molecule, the titanium (Ti) atom, being as an electron-rich centre, donates electron density to the antibonding orbital of hydrogen (H), leading to chemisorption of H2 which results in the dissociative activation of the H2 molecule. This activation arises from a charge transfer mechanism involving the transfer of electrons from the Ti dz 2 orbital to the vacant pz-orbital of hydrogen. The resultant d-p mixing facilitates the strong binding of the first H2 molecule. However, subsequent H2 molecules exhibit relatively weaker van der Waals interactions, resulting in their quasi-molecular adsorption on the Ti atom. The maximum limit of hydrogen molecules that could be quasi molecularly adsorbed was found to be three molecules.

Also after the chemisorption of the first H2 molecule, the adsorption energy values of the subsequent molecules fall in the quasi-molecular range and this is validated by noting the HH bond distances in all these molecules which are given in the Supplementary Table S.T.4. It can be clearly seen from the Supplementary Table S.T.4 that the HH bond lengths in the second, third and fourth adsorbed H2 molecules lie around 0.83–0.84 Å. Further, the TiH bond distances for all these three H2 molecules are between 1.90 and 1.92 Å. But it is not guaranteed that the H2 molecules will be adsorbed molecule by molecule in the same sequence. Hence average adsorption energy (
E ads avg
) was calculated for Ti_H-GQD24 and Ti_OH-GQD24. For calculating the average adsorption energy per H2 molecule, Ti_H-GQD24 and Ti_OH-GQD24 with one chemisorbed H2 were considered as the initial systems and the adsorption energy of the last three hydrogen molecules evaluated. The
E ads avg
for Ti_H-GQD24 and Ti_OH-GQD24 were found to be −0.485 eV and − 0.444 eV (see Table 7 ) respectively, which falls within the quasimolecular adsorption range. Thus, while the first H2 molecule chemisorbed on Ti adatom, the next three molecules adsorb on the Ti molecule in a quasi-molecular mode.

Coming to larger GQD sizes considered in the study, the adsorption energies for Ti atom on H-GQD40, OH-GQD40, H-GQD42 and OH-GQD42 are −4.1 eV, −3.87 eV, −3.95 eV and − 3.85 eV, respectively, validating their practical feasibility and stability. Continuing the evaluation of Ti_f-GQDs40 and Ti_f-GQDs42 for hydrogen molecule adsorption reveals consistently the trend of chemisorption of the first hydrogen molecule as seen from Fig. 6(b) and (c). This is noted for both Ti_H-GQDs and Ti_OH-GQD. The lowest adsorption energy is noted for the Ti_H-GQD40. Optimised structures of Ti_f-GQD40 and their H2 adsorbed counterparts are given in the Supplementary Fig. S.F.22. Optimised structures of Ti_f-GQD42 and their H2 adsorbed counterparts are given in the Supplementary Fig. S.F.23. We conducted H2 adsorption simulations in both parallel and perpendicular orientations. However, in both approaches, we observed that H2 adsorbs onto the Ti atom with a consistent orientation, specifically adopting a side-on orientation, and exhibits the same energy profile in both cases. It is clearly noted from these figures that the first hydrogen molecule undergoes a chemisorption followed by the quasi-molecular adsorption from second to the fourth hydrogen molecule.

Analysis of the HH bond lengths from Supplementary Table S.T.4 reveals that chemisorption of first hydrogen molecule in all cases with the exception of Ti_H-GQD40 is corroborated by an increased bond length of HH bond from 0.75 Å to a value greater than 2.5 Å. In case of Ti_H-GQD40, the interatomic distance between HH is slightly shorter, around 0.91 Å. However, as seen from the Supplementary Figs. S.F.22 and S.F.23, in all cases the first H2 molecule undergoes a cleavage with the 2H atoms anchoring in a dissociated mode on Ti atom. The adsorption energy of the second H2 molecule adsorbed on Ti_H-GQD40 is slightly higher than the quasimolecular range, but is less than that for chemisorption. Also the HH bond length indicates that the molecule is not dissociated. This shows that the second molecule just shows quasi-molecular adsorption. Thus, the second to fourth H2 molecules adsorb in a quasi molecular mode as noted from the pictorial representations in Supplementary Figs. S.F.22, S.F.23 and the HH bond lengths given in Supplementary Table S.T.4. Further all these molecules are distributed in a uniform manner in the first sphere around the Ti atom as noted from the TiH bond distances given in Supplementary Table S.T.4. All these factors, followed by the adsorption energies of second to fourth H2 molecules on the Ti atom (See Figs. 6(b) and (c)) demonstrate the quasi-molecular adsorption of H2. Supplementary Table S.T.4 gives the IR stretching frequency of the quasi-molecularly adsorbed H2 molecules. The frequency of the H2 molecules is in the range of 3000 cm−1 to 3300 cm−1 which is less than the frequency of the free standing H2 molecule (4400 cm−1). This decrease in the stretching frequency is consistent with the elongation of HH bond length and confirms that the H2 molecule is partially activated upon quasi-molecular adsorption. For the 2nd H2 molecule adsorbed on the Ti_H-GQD40 the IR frequency is below 3000 cm−1 (see Supplementary Table S.T.4). This is consistent with the longer HH bond length of the H2 molecule and higher adsorption energy. This confirms that the 2nd H2 molecule is adsorbed quasi-molecularly on the Ti_H-GQD40. Thus, the change in adsorption energy with respect to the number of H2 molecules in Ti_f-GQD40 and Ti_f-GQD42 follow a similar trend as noted for 24 atom GQDs. Further, the maximum limit of adsorbed H2 molecules is found to be 4. The
E ads avg
for the last three H2 molecules (leaving out the chemisorbed one) was calculated and it falls within the quasimolecular range for Ti decorated f-GQD40 and f-GQD42 as seen from Table 7.

Finally, the weight %(% w/w) of hydrogen adsorbed for all the Ti_f-GQDs is calculated and summarised in Table 8 . The weight % is calculated with respect to the three H2 molecules that can be stored reversibly due to quasi molecular interactions. The study reveals that all the Ti decorated f-GQDs can store a maximum of 4 H2 molecules. Thus Ti_OH-GQDm due to their high molecular weight have low % w/w. Ti_H-GQD24 due to its lowest molecular weight has the highest weight % of hydrogen storage of 1.7 % w/w. Thus Ti_H-GQD24 can be used as a potential hydrogen storage material.

In order to get a deeper understanding of the mechanism of hydrogen adsorption, electronic structure analysis is performed. Mulliken population analysis is done to understand the charge transfer during hydrogen adsorption. Table 9 gives the charge distribution on the GQD before and after adsorption of the first H2 molecule. It is noted from column 1 of the Table 9, that all the carbon atoms carry together a cumulative negative charge with Ti atom being a positive centre. For example, the total charge on Ti_f-GQD24 (which is inclusive of terminal H atoms) is −0.37 e and a same amount of positive charge is present on the Ti adatom. On adsorption of the first H2 molecule, the cumulative negative charge on the GQD atoms decreases as can be seen from the second column of the Table 9. Thus, on the chemisorption of the first hydrogen molecule on the Ti atom there is a charge redistribution with a total charge transferred to the H2 atoms from the GQD as seen from Table 9. Between 0.2 and 0.3 e are gained by the H atoms that chemisorb on the Ti atom. The charge on the Ti atom is more or less similar to its pre-adsorption values, with the charge loss mainly happening from the GQD surface through the Ti adatom. It is pertinent to note that a similar charge transfer from H2 molecule to GQD substrate during dissociative adsorption was noted earlier where Al clusters anchored on graphene sheets [91]. In order to check the validity of the mulliken population analysis, bader charge for the Ti_H-GQD24 was calculated as it is the smallest of the systems studied. The bader charge analysis showed that the charge on the GQD decreases in the negative value upon chemisorption of H2 molecule. This is in agreement with the Mulliken population analysis thus confirming its validity.

The subsequent H2 molecules do not dissociate on the Ti atom but undergo a quasi-molecular adsorption as noted earlier. Supplementary Table S.T.5 gives charge analysis on the Ti adatom as a function of the number of H2 molecules adsorbed on it. From the Supplementary Table S.T.4, it was earlier noted that after adsorption of the second, third and fourth H2 molecules, their HH bond length increases from the equilibrium length 0.75 Å to the range of 0.8 to 0.84 Å. This increase in bond length is more than that for physisorption, but the H2 molecule is still not dissociated. This elongation of bond length suggests that there is Kubas interaction [79]. In order to confirm the presence of Kubas interaction between H2 molecule and the Ti atom Mulliken charge analysis is done. The results show that there is a charge(−ve) transfer from Ti atom to H2 molecule. The charge transfer from Ti atom to H2 molecules after adsorption is given in Supplementary Table S.T.5. This charge transfer results in the elongation of HH bond length. A full Mulliken charge analysis reveals that there is an increase in the 3d orbital occupation of the Ti atom. Supplementary Table S.T.5 gives the Ti 3d orbital occupation after the adsorption of every H2 molecule. Fig. 7(a), (b) and (c) show that there is an increase in the occupation of the 3d orbital of Ti atom as a function of the number of H2 molecules for all three Ti_f-GQDsm. This shows that there is charge transfer from the H2 molecule to the empty 3d orbitals of Ti atom upon adsorption. Such type of charge transfer to the d orbital of Ti atom confirms the presence of Kubas interaction [77]. The adsorption process which is intermediate between physisorption and chemisorption is suitable for reversible hydrogen storage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Increasing the hydrogen storage capacity by increasing the number of Ti atoms
Increasing the concentration of Ti ad-atoms on H-GQDm (m = 24,40,42)
ce:section.ce:para: Since each Ti atom can adsorb 4 H2 molecules, H-GQD40 and H-GQD42 should adsorb minimum 6 Ti atoms to achieve the target of 5.5 % w/w of hydrogen storage. Thus, 6 Ti atoms were initially adsorbed on H-GQD40(6Ti-GQD40) and H-GQD42(6Ti-GQD42) three on each side. The initial distance between the Ti atoms was kept similar to that of 4Ti-GQD24. But the Ti atoms agglomerated upon optimization. In the case of 6Ti-GQD42 Ti3 complex was formed on both the surfaces of the GQD. The optimised structure of 6Ti-GQD42 is given in Supplementary Fig. S.F.24. The GQD is buckled, losing its planar structure after the adsorption of 6 Ti atoms. The TiTi bond distance in the Ti3 complex is 2.55 Å. In the case of 6Ti-GQD40 one Ti atom was adsorbed without agglomerating whereas two Ti atoms agglomerated to form Ti2 complex on each side of the GQD. There is a slight deviation in the planar structure of the 6Ti-GQD40 upon adsorption of 6 Ti atoms shown in Supplementary Fig. S.F.24. These results prove that the H-GQD40 and H-GQD42 cannot anchor increased concentrations of Ti atoms and consequently cannot be applied for higher storage capacity of molecular hydrogen.

To confirm the presence of Kubas type of interaction the partial density of states (PDOS) of the complex (4Ti_GQD24 plus the adsorbed H2 molecules) was done. The PDOS was calculated by using Vienna Ab-initio Software Package (VASP). The graph of the PDOS is given in Supplementary Fig. S.F.25. It can be seen from the figure that there is a significant overlap between Ti-3d states and the H-1 s states just below the fermi level. This suggests that there is a charge transfer from Ti-3d to the H-1 s that results in the elongation of the HH bond (Table 10). Also there is a slight overlap between Ti-3d and H-1 s above the fermi level. This confirms the presence of Kubas interaction as the overlap of states above the fermi level indicated charge transfer from H-1 s to the empty Ti-3d orbitals. This unique donor acceptor cooperative interaction is termed as η2 coordination mode (Kubas interaction) where hydrogen atoms from the H2 molecule donates electron density to the titanium atom forming a sigma bond and a π back donation from the filled orbitals of Ti atom to H2 orbitals [99]. Also it can be seen from Fig. 8.b that none of the H2 molecules is dissociated upon adsorption. Thus 4Ti-GQD24 can store 16 H2 molecules through quasimolecular interaction. The weight % of the hydrogen storage was calculated using Eq. (5). The calculated weight % is 6.1 % w/w. This is higher than the target of 5.5 %. Therefore 4Ti-GQD24 can be used as a potential medium for reversible hydrogen storage.

Born Oppenheimer molecular dynamics (BOMD) simulations were carried out on H2 molecules adsorbed 4Ti_GQD24 system in order to study the thermal stability of the system. BOMD simulations were carried using the deMon2k code in the NVT ensemble with a time step of 1 fs. The Nose-Hoover thermostat was used to maintain constant temperature [100]. Simulations were for a time period of 10 ps after 5 ps of equilibration time. The angular momentum of the system was held to be zero during the trajectories. The simulation was carried out at the desorption temperature which was calculated using the van't Hoff equation which is given below.
(8)
T d
=
E b
k b
∗
ΔS R
− lnP
− 1
where Eb is the binding energy of H2 molecule with 4Ti_GQD24 (−0.59 eV), kb is the boltzmann constant (8.617 * 10−5 eV/K), ΔS is the change in entropy of hydrogen for phase transition from gas to liquid (130.7 J/K), R is the universal gas constant(8.314 J/K.mol) and P is the ambient pressure(1 atm). Thus the desorption temperature obtained by Eq. (8) and the values given above is ≈ 435 K. In the context of these simulations, during the 10 ps trajectory, the 4Ti_GQD24 support remains structurally stable, while H2 molecules begin to desorb with 6 molecules desorbing by 10 ps simulation time validating a reversible hydrogen storage behaviour of the 24 atom GQD. The conformation of the GQD with desorbing H2 molecules are elucidated in the Supplementary Fig. S.F.26 (a) and (b). We have also carried out the BOMD simulation for a time period of 500 fs at 500 K temperature in order to study the thermal stability of the system and desorption of H2 molecules, during the 500 fs trajectory 4Ti_GQD24 support remains structurally stable and 11 H2 molecules desorbed without agglomeration of Ti atom on the GQD surface as illustrated in Supplementary Fig. S.F.26 (c) and (d).

The above results indicate that 24 atom GQD, an experimentally viable material [48–52], as a potential material for H2 storage. However, we further compare our results with previously obtained adsorption capacities on various graphene based systems. One of the earlier studies has shown that the Nb transition metal atom as an ad-atom on graphene shows promising results for hydrogen adsorption [93]. The Nb atom was able to adsorb 6 H2 molecules which gives a 1.9 % w/w. Lithium, potassium and sodium atoms as adatoms on GQD were studied as potential hydrogen storage media. GQD with 37 rings was considered(C96H24) [101,102]. Each system was able to adsorb 12 H2 molecules giving 2 %, 1.96 %, and 1.9 % w/w for lithium, potassium and sodium atoms respectively. In one study C54H18 GQD was doped with 3 chromium atoms in order to enhance the storage capacity of GQD [103]. Each Cr atom was able to adsorb three H2 molecules giving a storage of 2.14 % w/w. The effect of nitrogen doping of the periodic graphene sheet on hydrogen storage capacity was studied [104]. The nitrogen doped graphene displayed a 1.5 % w/w. GQD was decorated by platinum and iron to evaluate its hydrogen storage capacity. A comparison of the hydrogen storage capacity of graphene and its derivatives decorated with metal adatoms showed that Pt decorated graphene displayed a hydrogen storage capacity of 0.15 % w/w, whereas Fe decorated graphene increases the storage capacity to 2.15 % w/w [105]. In the present work Ti as an adatom on GQD(all the studied GQD systems) is able to adsorb 4 H2 molecules, out of which one is chemisorbed and three are stored reversibly. Out of all the GQDs considered Ti_H-GQD24 has the highest weight % of 1.7 % w/w. Thus in order to achieve the target of 5.5 % w/w the number of Ti atoms were increased. H-GQD24 can adsorb a maximum of 4 Ti atoms and can reversibly store 16 H2 molecules. Thus the weight % finally achieved was 6.1 % w/w which is much higher than all the previous results reported as mentioned in Table 8.
Table 11
Comparison of the hydrogen storage capacity of graphene based systems as noted from the earlier works. * Present work results referred to as for a single Ti adatom based system. ** Refers to 4Ti-GQD24 results obtained in the present work.
Table 11
Graphene based system studied earlier Maximum H2 wt% recorded Reference
Nb adatom on c-coronene ∼1.9 %(6 H2 molecules) [93]
Lithium as adatom on GQD(C96H24) ∼ 2 %(12 H2 molecules) [94]
Potassium as adatom on GQD ∼ 1.96 %(12 H2 molecules) [101]
Sodium as adatom on GQD ∼ 1.9 %(12 H2 molecules) [102]
3 chromium atoms doped on GQD(C54H18) ∼ 2.14 %(9 H2 molecules) [103]
Nitrogen doped graphene ∼ 1.5 % [104]
Graphene Sheet decorated with Fe ∼ 2.16 % [105]
Potassium decorated γ-graphene ∼ 13.95 % [106]
Present work* ∼ 1.7 %
Present work** ∼ 6.1 %

In search of 2D systems that can adsorb/store hydrogen in the quasi-molecular range, in the present study, we have analysed the adsorption capacity of hydrogen molecules on doped and decorated graphene nanoflakes with different sizes and with different edge functionalization by means of a first principles based method. The study reveals that adsorption energy of the H2 molecules for all the doped GQDs is not in the required range. However, only for Ti-decorated GQDs, the adsorption energies are in the quasi-molecular range. A single Ti atom decorated GQD can adsorb upto 4 H2 molecules of which the first H2 molecule is chemisorbed and the subsequent H2 molecules are quasi-molecularly adsorbed. A Kubas interaction between Ti atom and quansi-molecularly adsorbed H2 molecules is noted. The study also demonstrates that when 4 Ti atoms are adsorbed on H-GQD24 each Ti atom adsorbs 4 H2 molecules. In this case all the H2 molecules are adsorbed reversibly via Kubas type interaction. Thus, the weight % increases to 6.1 % w/w thus achieving the target of 5.5 % w/w of hydrogen storage. Doped graphene sheets and graphene based quantum dots are being increasingly explored for their potential hydrogen storage properties [107,108]. The present work demonstrates that the Ti based graphene quantum dots are excellent prospective hydrogen storage materials for future experimental investigations.
